item.content:encoded: A college football SCP, love it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The journal entries are a definite high-point — I was interested in the way Roy, an otherwise mundane person, dabbled in the occult and got, expectedly, burned. This is a neat, absurd twist on "grimdark". It comes together by the end to paint a pretty humorous picture.

My issue is with the way the article is structured. For one, the Description section is a slog to get through. I didn't think any single anomalous effect of Roy (well, Roy under the influence of a football poltergeist) was of particular interest to me, and I don't think I can remember what the anomalous properties were. Moreover, the SCP could have been stronger if Roy's fate — his possession — was not revealed until after "Document 1291-2", and then perhaps the "Binding Maintenance Procedures" would fit neatly at the article's end? In a way similar to how Potentially XK Tim Duncan is structured.

I know the SCP Format is restrictive of the way this story could be told. But, really, this is absurd in a good way, and if the premise was given room to shine, I really think it could!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A solid 'Mortals meddling with powers beyond their understanding' story with an oddly believable angle (from what I understand most college football coaches would happily sell their soul to win). Was the library the Wanderers Library? I'm relatively new to the Foundation so apologies if that's a silly question. Also +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, I LOVE that you did a college football skip, but I have a few quibbles:

Each week of the NCAA Division I Football Bowl Subdivision,

Needs "season" in there.

The Mississippi River College Moccasins are under no circumstances to be allowed to play in a BCS bowl, or to leave NCAA Division I.

Dropping down to FCS would invalidate the terms too, wouldn't it? Not that it's too difficult to keep a team in FBS (see also: Kansas) but that should definitely be specified.

(What conference are these guys in? Mountain West? Sun Belt?)

As for the question you posed back in 2014… well, the BCS bowls technically still exist in the form of the playoff games, but "technically" isn't going to keep a demon bound. I have an absolutely terrible idea that no one will understand except college football fans. Would you be up for a collaboration? If so, drop me a PM.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why the hell did I ever downvote this? I love scips about people using magic to create really mundane outcomes. And that final line is great!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is awesomely weird. I love it. Upvote all the way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How did I not make that connection until reading this?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It takes more than fabric to blind this thing. That's just there to prevent direct exposure.
Congratulations.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Creepy football coach sorcerer. He sounds like something that'd come from my part of the world. Hell yeah! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Given that the coach is to always be blindfolded, does that mean that when he's brought to the stadium to watch the game, he's only listening to it instead?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If your description of Mr. Wilson's state of mind in the post about the song interpretation is valid for the article, perhaps he was able to convince the demon/spirit/shagnasty to alter the deal to conform to whatever the new championship standard is. (I am bad at the sports)

After some suitably destructive containment breach, of course.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, this article has a problem. BCS is being abolished. Now, this is great news in reality, since it was an awful system hated by all, but it poses a slight problem for this article, which is contingent on the BCS being a thing that exists.

Now, I wrote this so that it takes place in the past (see the coach's comment about the Saints winning a Superbowl), but even so, something must clearly have happened between then and now that the Foundation can allow the BCS system to be replaced.

I'm thinking about doing a tale to explain the discrepancy. Anyone have any thoughts on what could have happened?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just had a thought. The spirits/demons/Yog-Sothoth's kid nephews and nieces here…were they unable to help because they didn't understand the question? As in, when he said 'Restore the Moccasins', they were thinking more along the lines of literally restoring some manner of native tribe(I'm aware there aren't any known Native American tribes that went by the name Moccasin, but there's plenty of play with words going on here that I could see them assuming something quite different)? Necromantic restoration that is.

We're going to the Hunting Grounds. Break the seals! Release the Manitous! The age of Wendigo is upon us!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That actually fits remarkably well. I looked up the lyrics, so let's do a bit lot of analysis.

Line-by-line on the song
How long did this take again?

And the saints we see
Are all made of gold

As Wilson says in his journal, wish-granting beings have a hard time making the Saints win the Super Bowl (i.e. get gold). Going back, this is referring to when the demon tries to grant his wish of a the Moccasins, represented by the Saints, winning the BCS championship.

When your dreams all fail
And the ones we hail
Are the worst of all

The meaning here is clear. Though the Moccasins should have gone on to win the BCS, they did not. Wilson's dream of them doing so failed when he realized the price of success (demon breaks free, does Bad Things to world). Thanks to the Foundation's influence, and the demon's lack thereof, the Moccasins, who Wilson hails, are probably doing worse than they did before.

I want to hide the truth
I want to shelter you

But Wilson, to prevent the end of the world, is fighting the demon from within. He's keeping it from fulfilling its mission of having the Moccasins win the BCS.

No matter what we breed
We still are made of greed

Wilson wasn't really driven just by desire to right by his players. He wanted to go down as the coach of the best Moccasins team in history.

This is my kingdom come

Though Wilson is biologically no longer alive, he's not flown off to whatever afterlife awaits in the Foundationverse. Instead, he remains locked in a struggle with the demon, with no end in sight.

When you feel my heat
Look into my eyes
It's where my demons hide

An apt summary of the symptoms of his possession. He gives off copious quantities of heat, and looking into his eyes exposes you to the full mind-shattering effects of the demon within.

Curtain's call
Is the last of all
When the lights fade out
All the sinners crawl

If the demon can ever break free of Wilson's body, referred to here as "Curtain's call", it won't be much fun for anyone.

So they dug your grave
And the masquerade
Will come calling out

The masquerade, a clear reference to the Foundation, forever buried the Moccasins' playoff hopes, so as to prevent the apocalyptic scenario that will ensue should they win.

It's woven in my soul
I need to let you go

The demon is tightly bound to Roy Wilson, making him the only person with power over it. Realizing the potential consequences of his actions, Wilson now understands that he must let go of his attachment to the Moccasins to save the world.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I found his theme song.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
They think the demon will be freed, yes. What happens then is unclear. It might just go away, or it might destroy the planet. "Hell freezing over" is just a figure of speech. If anything, the opposite is closer to the truth as it concerns this thing.

There's a reason the spirits he conjured couldn't help the Moccasins.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is the Foundation preventing the Moccasins from winning because they think the demon will be freed if they win, or is the Foundation from some reason afraid of Hell freezing over?

I tell them I want the Moccasins restored, but no. That’s the one thing they can’t do. There’s got to be better than this at the far library.

Got one last book. They didn’t want to let me check this one out, but I insisted.

Were the spirits lying in order to leading him to that particular book, to get that particular demon summoned?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
*world implodes*

I think you just made canon happen

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Suggestion: SCP-231 is really the result of a similar, but sloppier ritual, and 110-Montauk is watching D-class play a really staged game of football. The Children of the Scarlet King are really University of Alabama fans.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
in my head theres a sick silk nail

oooooohhhhkaaaay. You win. Upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
in my head theres a sick silk nail
UNTIL we win the feathered eye will turn its gaze to the world
When the Moccasins win, hell will freeze over.

…..Chills…

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You can observe something without watching it — for example, observing parts of a song.

From what I can gather, "visual" is for SCPs that only work by being viewed, like SCP-099, whereas "observational" would be for SCPs like SCP-1663 — even if you learn of its existence by hearing about it (i.e., nonvisually) it still has an effect.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, that was actually something in the original version of this. Foundation personnel don't get to bet on the Mocassins games.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The demon/genie/spirit/shadowland beast/lesser great old one inhabiting him seems to be of the super-literal sort. He won't leave the corpse/host til' his end of the bargain is fulfilled. Which means he might be around so long as the Foundation can keep fixing the games. …

I would love to be a bookie with this info. I would make all the money.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Quick question:

Is the coach immortal, or will he eventually die of natural causes?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is just an absolute joy to read. Awesome work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What I more mean is that I would think that having "visual" would render moot the need for "observational".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Observational" is more general, and could include "visual", "auditory", "olfactory", etc. Basically, sensing the thing, with ANY of the senses, triggers it. Check out the Tag Guide for more/better detail. It's what I usually use if I'm iffy on whether to add a specific tag or not.

Edit: Weird. I could've sworn we had more. Maybe they got deleted or re-tagged as something else.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks. Aren't visual and observational redundant? I'll add entropic, certainly, since the thing in the coach is basically an entropy demon. Extradimensional maybe, religious, no.

EDIT: Huh. It's in the tag guide, but this is now the only article with the "entropic" tag.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Done and done. There were a few I contemplated but didn't include (entropic, extradimensional & religious), but since I don't know the whole backstory, I thought that decision should be left up to the author.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is pretty damn neat. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Can someone who knows current tag standards tag this, please? I'm out of practice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That part stood out to me as well. Great phrase.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The journal entries made this for me, but in this case I was kind of disappointed by that. The rest of the article is just really dry and seems too bare, with the only other part really standing out at me being the Moccasins necessities.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Feathered eye" makes me think of Quetzalcoatl. And Aztecan sports were rather more… bloody than football. Perhaps the only way for the team to win is to play more to the god/spirit/demon's strengths.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Sportsville Sports are the best participants at Sportsball. The certainly do make more winningnesses than the other participants.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wonder if he happened upon an orang-utan while in the Library?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: FOOBAW

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Things being possessed by demons is always fun. Especially when you incorporate sports.

Sports friends are the best friends.

Sports… sports… sports…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
this is great.
where did you come up with "in my head theres a sick silk nail" ?!?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fantastic. I'm really impressed how readable you made "coach gets possessed by demon so he can win at footbaw". +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay, I hope this version is workable, then. I need food.
